mission.title: Install and Configure MySQL Operator for Kubernetes (Oracle)
mission.description: The MySQL Operator for Kubernetes automates the management of MySQL InnoDB Cluster setups within a Kubernetes environment. It simplifies tasks such as deployment, scaling, and backup, making it ideal for teams looking to implement MySQL in a cloud-native way. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the MySQL Operator Helm repository to your local Helm configuration, allowing you to install the operator using Helm.

```bash

helm repo add mysql-operator https://mysql.github.io/mysql-operator/

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the official MySQL Operator Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install MySQL Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the MySQL Operator using Helm, creating a namespace for it in the process.

```bash

helm install mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --create-namespace

```

> This command deploys the MySQL Operator in the 'mysql-operator' namespace.

steps.title: Verify Operator Deployment
steps.description:
This step checks if the MySQL Operator is running correctly by querying the deployment status.

```bash

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> You should see the operator deployment with 1/1 replicas ready.

steps.title: Create MySQL Root User Secret
steps.description:
This step creates a Kubernetes secret that contains the credentials for the MySQL root user, which is necessary for managing the InnoDB Cluster.

```bash

kubectl create secret generic mypwds --from-literal=rootUser=root --from-literal=rootHost=% --from-literal=rootPassword: <REDACTED>'

```

> Replace 'sakila' with your desired root password.

steps.title: Define MySQL InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
This step defines a MySQL InnoDB Cluster custom resource that references the secret created earlier.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.4'
EOF

```

> This command creates a new InnoDB Cluster named 'mycluster'.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.4'
EOF

steps.title: Verify InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
This step checks if the InnoDB Cluster is created and running properly.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com -n mysql-operator

```

> You should see the InnoDB Cluster 'mycluster' listed.

uninstall.title: Uninstall MySQL Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the MySQL Operator and its associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all MySQL clusters managed by the operator, leading to potential data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl delete namespace mysql-operator

```

> This command uninstalls the MySQL Operator.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade MySQL Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your MySQL data. This step performs the upgrade and verifies the operator's status afterward.

```bash

helm upgrade mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> This command upgrades the MySQL Operator to the latest version.
> Verify that the operator is running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create InnoDB Cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the secret for MySQL root user is not created or is misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Ensure the secret 'mypwds' is created correctly with valid credentials.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'mysql-operator not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the MySQL Operator has not been installed or the namespace is incorrect.

**Fix:**
Check the installation command and ensure the 'mysql-operator' namespace exists.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient permissions'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The operator may not have the necessary RBAC permissions to perform actions.

**Fix:**
Review the ClusterRole and ensure it has the required permissions for the resources it manages.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'InnoDB Cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may happen if the MySQL pods are not running or are in a crash loop.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the MySQL pods for errors and ensure they are running correctly.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for MySQL 8.4 features and enhancements.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new MySQL 9.0 features, including improved performance and new configuration options.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: To upgrade from 8.4 to 9.0, ensure all clusters are backed up and follow the upgrade procedure.

resolution.summary: You should see the InnoDB Cluster 'mycluster' listed with the status 'Ready' when running 'kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com -n mysql-operator'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.4'
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources and the required tools installed.
